title: Theory of frozen flux in a narrow uniform superconducting strip after cooling in a small magnetic field
abstract: We analyze residual frozen flux in a long narrow superconducting strip cooled through its transition temperature $T_{c}$ in a small perpendicular magnetic field. This problem is relevant for the issue of trapped magnetic flux in superconducting electronic devices. During cooling, the low-temperature vortex configuration is formed at temperatures very close to $T_{c}$, where the flux density is determined by dynamic balance between the thermally-activated exits and entries of vortices over the geometrical energy barrier formed by the interaction with the strip edges and the Meissner screening current. In the field range between the minimum flux-expulsion field and the penetration field, the equilibrium flux density is finite due to thermal activation and rapidly decreases with decreasing temperature. During cooling, however, the escape rate decreases exponentially, and the vortex density falls out of equilibrium at a field-dependent freezing temperature $T_{\mathrm{fr}}$. We derive and solve the dynamic-balance equation for this process, which yields definite quantitative results for $T_{\mathrm{fr}}$ and the frozen vortex density. The relative freezing temperature $1\!-\!T_{\mathrm{fr}}/T_{c}$ exceeds the fluctuation width of the transition by a large logarithmic factor, rapidly increases when the magnetic field approaches the minimum flux-expulsion field, and logarithmically increases with decreasing cooling rate. The resulting frozen flux density has a very strong magnetic-field dependence which can be used to define the effective flux-expulsion magnetic field.
summary: This theoretical work models how magnetic flux gets trapped (frozen) in narrow superconducting strips during cooling. By solving the dynamic-balance equation for vortex movement, the authors predict the freezing temperature and the resulting trapped flux density. This provides a quantitative understanding of flux pinning relevant to superconducting electronics.
main_problem: The paper analyzes the phenomenon of residual frozen magnetic flux trapped in a narrow superconducting strip when cooled through its critical temperature in a small external magnetic field.
main_result: The freezing process is governed by a dynamic balance equation, yielding a freezing temperature ($T_{	ext{fr}}$) and a frozen flux density ($n_{	ext{fr}}$) that exhibit strong, predictable dependencies on the magnetic field and cooling rate.
method: The authors derive and solve the dynamic-balance equation describing the thermally-activated escape and entrance of vortices over geometric energy barriers.
model_or_system: The system is a long, narrow superconducting strip ($W \ll \Lambda$). The dynamics are modeled using vortex energy profiles and the master equation for flux density evolution.
key_observables: Frozen flux density ($n_{	ext{fr}}$), freezing temperature ($T_{	ext{fr}}$), and the effective flux-expulsion magnetic field ($H_{	ext{eff}}$).
important_parameters: The relative freezing temperature ($\epsilon_{	ext{fr}} = 1 - T_{	ext{fr}}/T_c$), the cooling rate ($R$), and characteristic energy scales like the Pearl energy ($E_P$) and the minimum flux-expulsion field ($H_e$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the narrow-strip approximation ($W \ll \Lambda$) and assumes the system freezes in regimes where thermal activation dominates the dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate vortex energy profiles, the reduced function determining the escape barrier, and the time evolution of the flux density during cooling.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (flux trapping) to deriving the dynamic-balance equation, solving it asymptotically, and finally calculating the quantitative dependence of the freezing temperature and density on external parameters.
why_interesting: The study of flux freezing and dynamic equilibrium in superconductors involves non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, critical slowing down, and barrier crossing phenomena, which share mathematical structures with open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Representative vortex energy profiles for a narrow strip, Eq. (4), for three magnetic fields, He, 3He, and Hc1 assuming the relation Hc1 = 4He. In the curve for H = 3He, the definitions of the exit and entry barriers, Uout and Uin are illustrated.

FIG. 2. Plot of the function fU(h) determining the escape barrier Uout in Eq. (9) defined by Eqs. (10) and (11). The gray long-dashed line shows the asymptotics for h →1 in Eq. (13). The dark gray short-dashed line shows the asymptotics for h ≫1 in Eq. (14).

FIG. 3. The illustrative time evolution of the flux density n(t) during cooling with constant rate following from Eqs. (22) and (23) (blue curve in the bottom plot). The top plot illustrates the corresponding time dependence of the reduced tempera- ture ε(t). For comparison, we also show by the brown line the equilibrium density at the current temperature neq[T(t)], Eq. (17), and the first term in Eq. (22) nin(t) due to the ini- tial condition (cyan line). The arrow marks the location of the freezing time scale tfr = 2tm, where tm is determined by Eq. (26).

FIG. 4. The dependences of the reduced crossover magnetic field hs =Hs/He limiting the single-vortex regime of freezing, Eq. (36), on the reduced cooling rate Rτ0/εf for different values of the ratio 2w√εf/ξGL.

FIG. 5. Representative dependences of the reduced freezing temperature εfr and the frozen flux density nfr on the magnetic field in units of the flux-expulsion field for 2w√εf/ξGL = 2 and several values of the normalized cooling rate Rτ0/εf. The circles in the left plot mark the locations of the field hs limiting the single-vortex freezing regime, see Eq. (36) and Fig. 4.
